narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your journey toward law school, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to passing the bar exam as riskier than we'd like—landing at a 55% chance of failure that tipped the scales against your application. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the strongest signal was your Year 3 decile of 1.0, placing you at the very bottom of your class that year, which painted a picture of struggles mounting over time and pulled hardest toward this concerning outcome. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 4.0, showing early grades that weren't quite strong enough to build a solid foundation, reinforcing the model's worry about academic momentum. Your LSAT score of 34, while respectable, didn't stand out as a game-changer in this mix, adding another nudge toward caution. Coming from an upper-middle family income bracket factored in too, as the model associates that with certain patterns of preparation that didn't align with bar success here. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 echoed those earlier academic hurdles, layering on more doubt. Even elements like being a white female pursuing full-time studies played subtle roles, each tilting the story a bit more toward potential pitfalls. It's not that any one thing doomed your chances—it's this combination that made the model see a tougher road ahead, one where too many pieces pointed to falling short on the bar. But here's the empowering part: this is just a snapshot based on patterns from thousands of students like you, and with targeted steps—like bolstering those later-year grades or prepping intensely for the LSAT—you could rewrite your own ending. You've got the drive; now channel it into what you can control. We're rooting for you to prove this prediction wrong.
